data.title: Hints Of U.S. Treasury Intervention To Support The Yen
data.description:
The Man Who Helped Break The Bank Of England May Save The Bank Of Japan

Before becoming U.S. Treasury Secretary, Scott Bessent built his reputation in the hedge fund world. Early in his career, he rose to prominence at Soros Management, where he contributed to the firm’s legendary bet against the British Pound

Using Bessent’s research that the pound was overvalued, Soros Management made a $10 billion wager that the Bank of England would not continue to support their currency. It all came to a head on Wednesday, September 16, 1992, when the Bank of England abandoned using reserves to buy pounds, causing the currency to crash. Bessent’s firm made a tidy $1 billion profit.

This period is famously referred to as “Black Wednesday,” when Soros Management “Broke the Bank of England.”

I highlight Bessent’s risk taking nature in my recent Seeking Alpha article “Secretary Bessent Is Running The U.S. Treasury Like A Hedge Fund.”

Well, Bessent is back in the currency markets again. Only this time, as U.S Treasury Secretary, he is aiding the Bank of Japan ("BOJ") in supporting their currency.

The BOJ has a huge problem. They have been very slow in normalizing their monetary policy after an extended period of extreme easing. They are dealing with a delicate balance between new fiscal policy initiatives to prop up the economy while inflation remains persistently high. This has resulted in rising bond yields and a weak yen.

Possible Yen Intervention

There has been a flurry of activity in Japanese markets this past week. Prime Minister, Sanae Takaichi, who has only been in office for three months, proposed a new fiscal measure to temporarily eliminate the 8% consumption tax on food for two years. While this measure is appealing to voters who are dealing with the high cost of groceries, it creates a huge hole in the budget, with no clear solution as to how the tax cut would be funded.

Market concern that the revenue loss would be filled by new government borrowing caused long bond yields to spike, as investors worried that new debt would only increase Japan’s bloated borrowing. At a total debt to GDP level of over 250%, Japan is already the most indebted country in the world.

The market uncertainty was exacerbated by Takaichi calling for a snap election to be held on February 8th, only 18 days away. By dissolving the Lower House, whose term was to run until October 2028, the Prime Minister is hoping to capitalize on her popularity by strengthening her party and its coalition’s position in the parliament.

The foreign exchange value of the yen weakened leading up to these events.

On Friday, the BOJ announced that in an 8-1 vote, they decided to hold the policy rate at the current 0.75%. The lone dissenter voted to raise the policy rate an additional 25 basis points to 1.00% due to his concern about the elevated level of inflation.

At the post-meeting press briefing, BOJ Governor Kazuo Ueda was noncommittal about when the next rate hike might occur. With regard to bonds though, Ueda stated “Long-term interest rates are rising at quite a fast pace” and that “we are ready to take nimble action to cope with exceptional, unusual moves.”

As seen in the intra-day chart below, initially, the yen weakened to 159.25, on concern the BOJ wasn’t tightening enough to combat inflation and the new fiscal stimulus.

This was the yen’s highest level since June 2024 when the BOJ last intervened to support the currency at 160.00. It then rallied unexpectedly in anticipation that another intervention might occur.

The rally was strengthened several hours later when in New York, buzz began spreading that the New York Fed, at the behest of the Scott Bessent’s Treasury, was doing a “Rate Check” on what levels they could purchase Japanese Yen.

A rate check is considered a high-level warning that the government is concerned about the exchange rate and that a currency intervention might occur.

Sometimes, though, a rate check can be used as a way to give notice of an undesirable move in the currency, and cause positioning to create a reversal, without actually intervening.

Although the Fed is authorized to intervene in the foreign exchange (FX) markets, it is a rare event. There have only been three FX interventions in the past 30 years. The most recent was in 2011. Usually, the intervention is conducted in coordination with the central bank of the target currency.

At the end of the day, between the speculation in Japan about currency intervention, and the buzz in New York about the Treasury’s rate check, the yen rallied 1.75% to 155.63, This was the biggest 1-day surge since August.

Bigger Picture

Japan, like the rest of the G-7 economies, uses a target inflation rate of 2.0%.

CPI ex fresh foods and energy, otherwise known as Core Core CPI, is the BOJ’s preferred measure of inflation. As seen in the chart below, it broke above the desired 2.0% rate in October 2022. It has been above target ever since. Core Core for December 2025 came in at 2.9%.

While the Federal Reserve began Quantitative Tightening in March 2022l, the BOJ has been behind the curve. It took the BOJ an additional two years to follow suit. BOJ was the last man standing with their Negative Interest Rate Policy, which finally ended in March 2024.

Since then, the two central banks have been moving in opposite directions. The BOJ has raised their policy rate 4 times by a total of 85 basis points to the current level of 0.75%. Meanwhile, over the past 16 months the Fed has cut rates 6 times by a total of 175 basis points, to the current level of 3.75%. The yield spread differential between the BOJ’s and the Fed’s policy rates, at 300 basis points, is the narrowest it has been since August 2022.

On the longer end, Japanese Government Bond (JGB) yields have risen more significantly. The following chart shows the JGB yield curve when the BOJ ended their Negative Interest Rate Policy and today. While 3-month securities have risen by 79 basis points, 10-year JGB yields have risen 153 basis points and 30-year JGB yields are up 181 basis points.

The 10 yr JGB hit 2.38% this week, a 27-year high, and the 30 yr JGB set a new record at 3.92%. Additionally, 40-year JGB yields rose above 4.00% for the first time since they were introduced in 2007. Long yields surged as much as 35 basis points on Tuesday when the market was spooked by PM Takaichi’s stimulus package of spending increases and the consumption tax cuts, as well as her call for the snap election on February 8th.

Treasury Secretary Bessent Ties Rise In Treasury Yields To JGB Selloff

Speaking from Davos, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said that he believed the recent weakness in Treasury bonds is tied to the selloff in JGBs.

He downplayed that the threats from Denmark of selling their Treasury securities in protest of the US position on Greenland were of any significance. While Bessent was curt with his views on Denmark, he was not wrong. Denmark only holds $10 billion in Treasury securities, a mere 0.1% of the $9 trillion of Treasury securities held by foreigners.

What went unsaid, however, was the risk of Japan selling their Treasury holdings in order to support their currency. Compared with Denmark, Japan is a very significant owner of Treasury bonds. Their holdings of $1.2 trillion in Treasuries make then the largest foreign owner of Treasury securities.

Perhaps Bessent is helping out the BOJ through Treasury’s rate check, so they don’t have to sell their Treasuries.

We know Bessent is worried about the JGB influence on Treasuries, because a key component of his strategy at Treasury for overweighting Treasury bill issuance, is to have longer rates come down, in parallel, with short rates. That way Treasury can term out their debt issuance into longer maturities at lower rates.

So far that hasn’t happened.

Since the Fed began lowering the Fed Funds Rate in September 2024, there have been six rate cuts totaling 175 basis points in rate reduction, to 3.75%. During that same period, the yield on the 10-year Treasury note (US10Y) has risen 63 basis points to 4.26%.

While at the beginning of the period we had an inverted 10-year Treasury to Fed Funds spread, by -187 basis points, it has now flipped to be positively sloped by 51 basis points.

Deteriorating Liquidity In The JGB Market

One of the problems in the JGB market is that no one wants to own them.

The largest holder of JGBs is the BOJ, and they are paring their positions through their version of Quantitative Tightening. In 2010, the BOJ owned less than 10% of the JGB market, but over the ensuing 13-year period, when they were executing their aggressive Quantitative Easing policy, their JGB holdings grew dramatically. A key element of their aggressive easing was their Yield Curve Control Policy, where they continuously purchased JGB’s to defend the band around their 0.0% target yield for 10-year JGBs. These continuous purchases swelled the BOJ’s JGB position to 54% of total JGBs outstanding.

Since their QT program began, though, the BOJ’s JGB holdings have declined to a 50% share of the total outstanding. No one has stepped up to fill the void, hence the sharp rise in yields.

The deterioration in liquidity is best exemplified by the Bloomberg JGB Liquidity Index. The index measures the average yield difference to fair value. When the difference is small, the market is considered quite liquid. As the average yield widens from fair value, the index signals distressed conditions and a deterioration in liquidity.

During normal times, the index hovers around 1 basis point. Since the BOJ embarked on their QT program, and stopped buying JGBs, the index has deteriorated significantly. The bond market selloff described earlier, caused the index to spike to a record high of 10 basis points, before settling back to a still illiquid level of 8.7 basis points.

This is a tremendous source of concern now, as the Prime Minister’s new policies will cause debt issuance to increase into this illiquid market.

Conclusion

Treasury Secretary Bessent, who began his career making large currency bets by helping Soros Management “Break the Bank of England,” is returning to his hedge fund ways.

On Friday, at the behest of Secretary Bessent’s U.S. Treasury, the New York Fed conducted a rate check among currency dealers to determine where they might be able to buy yen. The rate check is a loud signal that they are not happy with the current exchange rates, and an indication that currency intervention might occur.

The rate check caused the yen to rally 1.75%, it’s largest one day move in five months.

Last week, while attending the World Economic Forum in Davos, Secretary Bessent indicated that he thought the weakness in the U.S. Treasury market had been impacted by the selloff in JGBs. He also stated that “I’ve been in touch with my economic counterpart in Japan, and I am sure that they will begin saying the things that will calm the market down.”

While President Trump has been railing about the Fed cutting the Fed Funds rate, Secretary Bessent has been more focused on the 10-year Treasury yield coming down, as that has more impact on mortgage rates, car loans and business lending.

The Treasury has made a big bet on this occurring, as it has been funding the government’s borrowing needs with Treasury Bills, because Bessent feels long rates are too high to term out the government’s borrowing, thereby locking in a high borrowing cost.

This hasn’t happened yet, though, as while the Fed Funds Rate has declined by 175 basis points since the Fed began easing, the 10-year Treasury yield has actually risen by 63 basis points.

By conducting a rate check on the yen, the Treasury is helping the BOJ support their currency during a period of internal turmoil in Japan. But it is also moving to protect the Treasury market from possible sales of Treasury securities by Japan, the largest foreign holder of U.S. government debt. Another impact, of course, of weakening the dollar in support of the yen, is to improve our trade exports.

data.title: Cipher Mining: Execution Replaces Speculation In 2026
data.description:
Cipher Mining (CIFR) has been one of the Bitcoin miners executing well these past years. I've been long CIFR for the past few years. My past coverage of the company reflects this stance, which all carried Buy ratings. In between the coverages, there have been good runs and sometimes retracements. In my last coverage I highlighted the point that Cipher Mining’s rerating story isn't over yet.

CIFR is up a lot from 2024, but currently off 2025 highs. In this piece, I'd look at what lies ahead for 2026 as a Bitcoin miner and a HPC/AI play, and also touch on the HPC deals secured last year and the implication of those deals into the present thesis.

Cipher Mining - From Pipeline to Paychecks in 2026

Cipher Mining is one of the HPC/AI plays expecting an operational inflection point in 2026. The market at the moment has remained muted since the HPC/AI hype late last year for the miners that secured deals.

​They all rerated in Q4, but have now since mostly retraced those gains. CIFR itself surged to a 52-week high of ~$25 on the news of the $3 billion 10-year Fluidstack lease deal last September, then followed by the $5.5 billion AWS deal, before retracing following the issuance of senior secured notes totalling $1.73 billion in mid November.

I'd like to say that the market seems exhausted in assigning premium to miners turned HPC plays based on just announced deals, pipeline capacity, and projected cash flow discounted into today's share price. I highlighted these in an article published here on Seeking Alpha last Friday covering Hut 8 Corp. (HUT), another miner turned HPC play. While each of these miners have secured what looks like similar multi-billion HPC deals, nuances in the deals themselves, as well as each miner's equity composition (of which I'm currently weighing their specific exposure to Bitcoin volatility through their balance sheets), and projected net operating income [NOI] for the duration of the deal are the specific metrics I'm looking at as I analyze these companies.

​In my latest HUT article, I downgraded HUT from a Buy to a Hold, despite HUT having seen similar capacity development and deals win as the rest of the HPC pivot companies. The main reason was the longer-dated timeline for their River Bend site energization compared to peers, which was compounded by the company's exposure to Bitcoin as it holds over 13,000 BTC on the balance sheet. These present a double headwind for the company, making 2026 a potentially muted year for HUT.

​In contrast, as I analyze Cipher Mining going into 2026, I'd be maintaining a Buy for CIFR for the singular reason that the checks for the HPC lease from AWS are projected to hit the books from August this year, and 168 MW Fluidstack hosting capacity at Barber Lake expects to commence rent in September. Impressive top line numbers making the headline are a first place to watch for the next catalysts.

​I’ve talked about the sentiment of the current market, which has apparently moved beyond maintaining momentum based on just pipeline capacity or capacity under exclusivity. Those were last year's catalysts. 2026 is the execution year, when HPC pivots show actual top-line impact on financials.

To project what's coming for Cipher, I'd make some assumptions. Take the Cipher's base mining revenue to be ~$55 million per quarter (I derived that using the average revenue of three quarters already reported in FY25); annualized, this gives a steady mining run rate of ~$220 million. I'm quite positive the company can now produce that amount of quarterly revenue from mining as a steady run rate till the next halving, with current hashrate at 23.5 EH/s and mining efficiency looking good at 16.8 J/Th.

Now layer in the HPC contracts. Cipher has roughly $8.5 billion of contracted revenue from the AWS and Fluidstack deals. If this revenue is recognized on a straight-line basis over a 13-year weighted contract life, it implies ~$650 million in annual HPC revenue (calculated as $8.5 billion for the combined deal divided by 13 years). And would put total sales from both mining and HPC to about $870 million. Note that my estimate is still a conservative one as I use a straight line assumption for the modeling.

Cipher's adjusted EBITDA margin improved greatly in 2025. It was just around 12% in Q1 2025 on $49 million revenue and $6.1 million adjusted earnings, but jumped to ~60% in Q3 on a $72.0 million revenue and ~$41 million adjusted EBITDA in Q3. I believe higher adjusted EBITDA is sustainable because it was driven by the upgrade of the mining fleets to from older Bitmain T21s to S21 xp Antminers. Economies of scale are likely to stay intact in the meantime with factors like network difficulty and Bitcoin price not moving too much.

For the purpose of my modeling for projected earnings, I'd however factor in volatility and go with a conservative ~50% adjusted EBITDA for the mining operations moving forward, which I believe the company can sustain considering the improved 16.8 J/Th average efficiency the S21 xp Antminers has brought to the mining fleet.

Valuation is bound to shift from Cipher's current life as a Bitcoin miner and when the HPC checks hit in H2. In the deals, management is guiding for 80 - 85% NOI margin, which I believe is very well within range, as the AWS deal is a triple N, meaning the clients take care of the biggest three costs (power, cooling, and facility costs). For this modelling, I'll be treating NOI margin as EBITDA margin which is safe enough considering low overhead and other operational levers in the HPC lease deal, which when accounted for won't skew our EBITDA from NOI estimate materially.

Segment

Annual Revenue Projection ~

Adj. EBITDA Margin

Annual EBITDA Contribution

Bitcoin Mining

76.1% (Blended)

$662.5 Million

At $6.94 billion market cap at present and $870M projected 2027 sales, CIFR trades at ~7.9x sales, meaning at current market cap CIFR is trading around 7.9x projected sales. A ~7.9x forward sales multiple for a company with 75%+ blended EBITDA margins and contracted revenue from AWS and Google can be considered modest. Traditional data center providers like Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Reality (DLR) typically trade in the low mid teens to sales, but they have much slower growth rates as they are well established firms beyond the rapid growth phase. The case for CIFR is that it is combining high-margin HPC revenue with optionality in Bitcoin upside and potential for rapid growth, which are visible through the pipeline capacity and the AWS and Fluidstack deals themselves which have clauses that allow expansion down the line.

Using the current $6.78 billion EV and our blended EBITDA projection of $662.5 million from both segments, implies an EV/EBITDA [fwd] of 10.2x (gotten from EV divided by projected EBITDA), and the valuation gets even more attractive.

At 10.2x 2027 EBITDA, CIFR is being priced as if the HPC deals are still high-risk speculations. The market is ignoring the fact that the AWS and Fluidstack contracts are legally binding and the sites already in build-out, the market is underpricing the near certainty of the coming cash flows.

As the HPC revenues start to come in, if CIFR catches up to the Sales multiple of data center provider peers (moving from 8x to 10 - 12x), that would imply a ~25% upside for the rerating from current stock price. If CIFR catches up to the EBITDA multiple (moving from 10.2x to 20 - 30x), the stock would more than double.

Risks

The risk I worry about most at this stage is construction and energization slippage risk. As I have noted with Hut 8 and why I downgraded to a Hold because of longer-dated energization timelines, while peers are already locked in for HPC revenue just months away. The market punishes longer-dated timelines. Any hint that the August 2026 for AWS site launch and October for Fluidstack site launch is sliding into 2027 might trigger a sharp CIFR sell-off.

Takeaway

The market moved with momentum on the AWS deal news in November and is now under-appreciating it in January. The gap between current prices around $17 and the top last November around $25 is the execution premium that will likely return as we get closer to that first lease payment in 2H this year.

We can do as much rough math as we want, but unless the HPC lease payments don’t start hitting the top line in September/October, CIFR will likely trade as a momentum stock in the latter part of this year, to be driven by the headline top-line numbers from the HPC deals.

data.title: VIG: Dividend Investors Should Be Careful (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF Shares (VIG) has started 2026 with a gain of 1.5%, which is ahead of the S&P 500 increase of 1.1%. Since I initiated coverage almost three months ago, explaining why I don't dig VIG, it has gained 2.7%, while SPDR S&P 500 Trust ETF (SPY) has gained 0.6%. It's all good, right? No, as over the past year, returning 12.1% it has still performed worse than SPY and also Vanguard S&P 500 (VOO) , which have both returned 14.4%.

VIG tracks the S&P U.S. Dividend Growers Index, which will be rebalanced in March. The index does track large-cap stocks, so comparing the return to SPY or VOO does make sense. At year-end, the index had its top 10 names representing 35.1% of it. Standard & Poor's shares the breakdown by sector as of year-end:

It also makes sense to compare VIG to other dividend-focused ETFs. I include seven others on my ETF Watch List that totals 80 ETFs. I reiterated my bullishness on recently,and it is one of them. VIG has been the worst performer relative to these peers in January so far and the second-worst over the past three months. The worst has been Invesco High Yield Equity Dividend Achievers ETF (PEY).

In this follow-up to my initial review of VIG, I compare it to the peers and conclude that at this higher price, my rating should move from Sell to Strong Sell.

How VIG Has Performed

Here is the year-to-date action for VIG and seven other dividend-focused ETFs that I follow closely:

While it is up the least, it has outpaced the group substantially since the end of 2022:

As much as it is up relative to peers, it has lagged VOO, which has returned 88.0% since the end of 2022. I discuss the two at the bottom below as potential alternatives.

Looking at the VIG chart, it set an all-time high recently:

Since the low in April it has rallied 31.8% after the recent pullback. I see support at $215 and then $204. At the low in 2020, it traded below $90. A 38.2% retracement from the recent peak to that level would result in a drop to about $174, which is just above the April low and would be a decline of 22% from the current price.

The Challenges VIG May Face

When I initiated coverage in late October, I suggested that its large exposure to Technology had helped it do well. While 27% is lower than the S&P 500 exposure of 34.4%, it is quite high. If Technology, a big winner over the past three years pulls back, it could hurt VIG. More specifically, if the large Technology stocks that VIG holds go down, it could hurt the ETF. Among the top 10 names in the ETF at year-end were Broadcom (AVGO), Microsoft (MSFT) and Apple (AAPL), totaling 15.2% as the top three holdings. All three are down by more than 3.6% in 2026.

The low exposure to Utilities may hurt VIG too. I initiated State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) with a Sell, but moved it to Hold recently after it declined. My issue with the large-cap Utilities has been the market-cap weighted exposure to just a few of them. The good news is that VIG doesn't own the ones that concerned me. The challenge, though, and why I upgraded XLU to Hold from Sell, is that interest-rates could fall, which would perhaps shift demand away from more consumer-oriented sectors. The index that VIG is tracking is quite low in Utilities.

While Financials have gone up since I warned about State Street® Financial Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLF) in early June, they have certainly lagged. The risks that I pointed out for XLF remain, and VIG has a lot of exposure to large-cap Financials. Three of the top ten names are big Financials companies, and they are all down in 2026 by 7% or more. This is the second-largest part of VIG and is substantially higher than the S&P 500 exposure.

So, weakness in its two very large sectors could hurt VIG. Of course, we could see a rebound in these stocks, which have declined so far. The ETF has a very large-cap focus. Vanguard on the VIG page of its website shares that at year-end the median market cap of the ETF was $277 billion. This is smaller than VOO at $382 billion, but it is quite big. The year has started with smaller companies doing better than larger-ones, and it could keep happening. Over the past three years, the S&P 500 has returned almost three times the 31.6% return of the Russell 2000. If large-caps continue to underperform, it could weigh upon VIG.

Alternatives for Investors

Here is an update of the table I have shared for the dividend-focused ETFs:

VIG has an extremely low management fee and it is the largest of these dividend-focused ETFs in assets. Its return over the past year is near the high of the group, and its five-year return has been the best among them. It also has the lowest dividend yield, though it is a premium to the dividend yields of VOO and SPY.

When I wrote about it in December, I said that PEY was okay, and I rated it a Hold despite my bearishness. It has rallied 4.1% in total return compared to a slight decline in the S&P 500 since then. I pointed out that its management fee of 0.54% is much higher than VIG, but I think that it is not so high that investors should not consider it. The ETF is much smaller than VIG and holds just 50 stocks measured against a different index. After I wrote that piece, my wife added some to her IRA, and I included a 10% position in an ETF model portfolio that I created and shared at year-end.

While it is up a lot so far in 2026, I continue to like ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers ETF (SMDV), as I explained earlier this month. It is focused on high-quality small-caps and uses equal weighting rather than market-cap weighting. PEY is focused on large-caps, but SMDV small-cap focus may keep some investors away from it. My wife owns this one in her IRA too, and it is currently 15.4% of my ETF model portfolio that holds seven ETFs.

For investors looking at dividend ETFs, I do like these two that I have discussed here, though there may be some others that could make more sense. I view the dividend-yield of a stock as just one thing and would never prioritize that yield. I am not saying that investors should sell VIG because it is the lowest dividend yield of these eight. While I like PEY, it is not because of its higher yield. The one I like the most of these, SMDV, has a below-average dividend yield.

I currently have a 15% position in Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP) in that model portfolio and initiated it with a Hold rating at the end of November, calling it a unique and compelling ETF. It has outpaced SPY and VOO since then, and has returned 3.8% so far in 2026. RSP has a lot fewer Seeking Alpha followers at 21K. For those who care about the dividend yield, RSP is slightly higher than VIG.

Conclusion

12 weeks after I initiated VIG with a Sell rating, it has advanced slightly. I am downgrading VIG to Strong Sell. If readers own it, they can sell at a slightly higher price. While it has lagged some of the ETFs that make more sense, I am suggesting that these alternatives make sense.

data.title: Expedia: Strong Results, Improving US Demand, Raised Guidance, And Attractive Valuation
data.description:
We are returning to Expedia (EXPE) after roughly a year (Fig. 1). While we have not provided quarterly updates throughout the period, the company and its industry dynamics have remained under close observation, and we have recently published several related analyses, including: Trip.com (Antitrust Overhang Creates An Attractive Entry Point) and Tripadvisor (Strategic Pivot Underway, Compelling Upside Ahead).

Our buy case rested on 4 pillars:

  1. Expedia’s clear valuation gap versus peers (both on P/E and EV/EBITDA),
  2. evidence of better top-line traction helped by easier year-on-year comparisons,
  3. leadership refresh with a new CFO (this was also marked by Wall Street sentiment improvement towards the new management), and
  4. a balance sheet that remains lightly levered alongside continued share repurchases.

One year later, we report a total return of almost 58%. After a thorough sector review, also informed by McKinsey (The Evolving Role of Experiences in Travel), we still see upside ahead.

Why are we still positive?

Here at the Lab, the most meaningful sector takeaway came from OpenAI's latest change to ChatGPT's advertising. Over the last few months, we have come to understand that bears are countering the change in client interaction driven by AI disruption. With OpenAI starting to test ads in ChatGPT, we believe this implies that AI will no longer be disruptive, but will work more like Google. Thus, online travel platforms might potentially benefit from it. So, we currently view Expedia as a one-stop shop with supportive inventory fragmentation. By leveraging our cross-coverage on Trip.com and McKinsey report , the travel experience market could exceed $1 trillion, and Expedia adapted its search tool with Packages and Things to Do. With Expedia's data, the company will likely target younger demographics, taking advantage of consumer preferences for experiences and tourism at the expense of traditional goods ownership.

Fig 1

Looking at the Q3 results, Expedia room nights accelerated to +11% year over year (Fig. 2). This was backed by broad-based strength across regions. More importantly, Expedia reported better numbers than its peers over the same period. In numbers, Airbnb was up by +9% and Booking by +8%. As shown in Fig. 2, the B2B business remains the primary driver of consolidated growth, lifting the group’s overall trajectory by +26% year over year. In comparison, the B2C franchise also re-accelerated to +7% yearly, easing concerns that this segment is structurally rolling over. Additionally, and also aligned with inventory supply, the more important point is Expedia's progress in tightening its value proposition. Expedia is better aligning its product offering and merchandising across its core markets.

Source: Expedia Q3 2025 ppt - Fig 2

Given the positive results, during the analyst call, Expedia management confirmed that the US market is improving and Expedia is executing well into that tailwind. For this reason, the group raised the Fiscal Year 2025 guidance at +7% Gross Bookings and +6–7% top-line sales. This is above the initial +4–6% range communicated on the Q4 2024 call. Here at the Lab, aside from the guidance raise, we continue to favor Expedia given its durable formula (EPS growth > EBITDA growth> revenue growth - Fig. 3).

Fig 3

Someone could argue that B2C bookings' growth of 7% continues to lag behind B2B. Others are questioning the "moderated pace" margin expansion in Fiscal Year 2026. That said, we view this as overly conservative. From a bull-case perspective, Q3 checks the boxes, and Expedia continues to trade conservatively versus its direct peers. Room Nights reached 108 million, and there is potential for various growth initiatives in 2026. The peers' check below is supported by our recent assessment of Starboard Value’s presentation (Fig. 4). More importantly, Expedia's growth trajectory is higher than its peers.

Fig 4

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Even accounting for a 56.55% increase in stock price, we believe Expedia has upside potential. The company is consolidating its loyalty programs and tech stacks. This will improve retention and cross-selling and should drive benefits to its core operating profit margin. In our numbers, rolling forward our estimate to 2026, we project a 10% sales growth to $16.2 billion. Considering the higher 2025 guidance and this impressive B2B segment growth, we are confident Expedia will likely achieve an EBIT margin of at least 21%. Given a D&A of $750/$800 million, Expedia's 2026 EBITDA is forecasted at $4.15 billion. Given its current net debt position, Expedia has almost no financial obligations (Fig. 5), even after accounting for cash and short-term investments. So, with a current market cap of around 8.3x, which is not far from Starboard Value's valuation. Continuing to value Expedia at 11.0x EBITDA (unchanged from our previous valuation and in line with the company's historical average), we arrive at an equity value of $45.65 billion. This represents an upside of 32%. Our new target price is set at $328.5 per share.

Fig 5

Risks

Expedia’s downside cases include FX volatility, failure to leverage the fixed cost base, and wage inflation. The company might be impacted by higher marketing costs and less efficient marketing spend. Additionally, as reported in the analysis, AI remains a question mark for the travel experience, even though we are positive about it. The company is exposed to competition from major OTA travel companies such as Booking and Airbnb, as well as softer consumer demand. Google also might push further into online travel.

Conclusion

In a nutshell, we believe Expedia's shares still have upside in capital appreciation. We remain constructive on the travel sector, and Expedia remains inexpensive relative to its larger peers and has many opportunities to continue achieving a positive, profitable growth trajectory at double-digit rates.

data.title: ConocoPhillips Has Future Appreciation Potential
data.description:
ConocoPhillips (COP) is a major upstream-only oil company with a $120 billion market cap. The company is committed to future growth with the production of millions of barrels/day and a number of exciting capital projects, yet its share price has been relatively flat since we recommended investing last.

As we'll see throughout this article, the company is committed to future shareholder returns and growth, making it a valuable investment.

ConocoPhillips Quarterly Highlights

ConocoPhillips had a strong quarter with $2.5 billion in FCF for the quarter, with $5.4 billion in CFO and continued capex.

That puts the company at an 8% FCF yield, a reasonable FCF yield given the growth potential that the company has. The company's strong production has enabled it to come in at the high end of its guidance, pushing 2.4 million barrels/day in production. The company has continued to improve its asset base, and it's increased its dividend by 8%.

That means its yield at the time is ~3.2%. Strategically, ConocoPhillips has a number of expansion projects through Willow and PALNG. These projects will cost billions in capital but are exciting new opportunities. The company's outlook is for continued cost management with a focus on strong operations and incremental FCF going into 2029.

ConocoPhillips Growth Investments

ConocoPhillips is continuing to invest heavily in growth, despite rising costs.

The Willow Project is expected to cost $8.5-9 billion, of which more than half has been spent. The company spent $2 billion in 2025, and for 2026 to 2028, it's expected to be $1.7 billion/year before the project finishes up and comes online in 2029. However, with strong FCF, the project should still be able to justify itself as an investment.

For the company's LNG project, total capital has been reduced to $3.4 billion, with a $600 million PALNG credit. 80% of the LNG project is capital complete with only $0.8 billion of spending remaining. The company expects the first LNG for NFE in 2026. For reference, ConocoPhillips only has a 3.125% stake in the massive project.

ConocoPhillips is focused on a purpose-built LNG strategy with global scale. With NFE/NFS, the company's resource LNG is expected to grow to 10 MTPA, with both resource and commercial LNG growing in kind to 2032. North American gas is valuable, low-cost gas that can go through other markets, with ConocoPhillips controlling a valuable supply chain.

ConocoPhillips 2026

Looking at ConocoPhillips for 2026, the company has substantial potential to drive shareholder returns.

ConocoPhillips is continuing to manage its capital spending with a substantial portion of growth projects already paid. 2026 capital guidance is $3 billion/quarter, down by $400 million/quarter from 2024. At the same time, the company is managing its core costs, with $10.2 billion in 2026 guidance down $1 billion from 2024.

Despite all of this, ConocoPhillips is guiding for 0-2% production growth. It outperformed the midpoint of guidance in 2025, and it'll expect modest growth in 2026 with manageable capital and costs.

Going from 2026 to 2028, ConocoPhillips is expecting ~$1 billion per annum of improvement in FCF. The company has new LNG startups and cost/margin enhancements along with a massive jump in 2029 as the 180 thousand barrels/day Willow project starts up with a 30-year reserve life, boosting the company substantially.

At current WTI prices, that means ~$6 billion in incremental FCF. That puts the company at $16 billion in 2029 FCF, a comfortable 13% FCF yield in just a few years from those who invest today. That will enable the company to repurchase shares and increase dividends, generating strong shareholder returns.

ConocoPhillips Shareholder Returns

ConocoPhillips ability to generate shareholder returns is clearly visible.

ConocoPhillips has earned $6.1 billion in YTD FCF along with $2 billion from working capital and dispositions. That's because the company has had a substantial $9.5 billion in capital expenditures. After that, the company has spent $7.9 billion on shareholder returns ($10.5 billion annualized). That's a high single-digit FCF yield, one that the company can comfortably afford.

The company finished with slightly more cash than it started with. This shows the company's commitment to valuable long-term returns.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is crude oil prices. As seen above, the company's $7 billion in incremental FCF improvement drops to $6 billion with WTI dropping to $60/barrel. With the potential for weaker oil prices and ongoing trade wars and economic issues, that could substantially hurt ConocoPhillips ability to drive future shareholder returns.

Conclusion

ConocoPhillips is a strong, pure-play upstream company, with a market cap of just over $120 billion. The company has continued to generate strong cash flow, focusing on share buybacks and dividends, with a high single-digit dividend yield. The company has done that while being in a phase of investing massively in its business.

However, the company is going through an inflection point. FCF is expected to expand by roughly $1 billion/year for the next several years before spiking by a massive $4 billion in 2029, supported by the Willow Project startup. That combination of factors makes ConocoPhillips today a great investment. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Streamex: Why The Yield On Gold May Not Justify The Premium
data.description:
In this article, I will cover Streamex Corp. (STEX). I will talk about why I think that their business model has flaws and that they are overvalued at the current price. I will also talk about the lockup period expiring in March and how that might provide some downward pressure.

Introduction

As usual, I will start by talking about their business model. Streamex's main business is providing a digital asset named GLDY that gives the investor exposure to the upside (downside) of the price of gold while earning an interest yield. STEX advertises a yield of up to 4%. This is done on the Solana blockchain. STEX lends this digital gold to their partner, Monetary Metals who in turn leases it to the commercial businesses. In general, we could say STEX serves as a liquidity provider to Monetary Metals and in return STEX earns a fee on the interest that Monetary Metals receives.

Estimating STEX’s potential revenues

I tried to find the interest rate that Monetary Metals charges the customer, yet since they are a private company, they don’t publicly disclose this information. Since GLDY token holder would get an advertised yield of 4%, Monetary Metals has to lease (digital) gold for a higher yield. Usually, high quality borrowers have access to prime capital markets, like the London gold market. Such markets are dominated by the large banks, for example London gold market is controlled by the ‘’Big four’’. According to the Discovery Alert, lease rates offered by these big banks are between 2-4% APR. This creates a problem, first large businesses such as national mints, or large jewelry producers would prefer to borrow from well-known financial institutions instead of a small unknown player such as Monetary Metals. Second since the yield on GLDY is (up to) 4% such leases become unprofitable.

The only way to get higher yield is to lease to riskier borrowers, who would get rejected by the big banks. In my opinion these are borrowers from developing countries. Based on the Teji Mandi Report from February 2025 interest rates in the markets like India reached an all-time high of 6-7% in 2025. Such rates would be profitable for Monetary Metals, giving 4% to the token holders while splitting the rest between STEX and themselves. Based on the 6-7% interest yield, what remains is 2-3%. I will estimate that Monetary Metals gets the majority since they handle the customers, insurance etc. Thus, what remains is 1%, and these are the total revenues that STEX makes from the gold that is leased.

I would also like to mention the transfer and tokenization fees. If an investor holds the GLDY token, these are not recurring fees, so in my opinion, these are less important than the lease spread; thus, I will concentrate on that recurring revenue. Still, in the latest earnings call, management said ''So if you had just a $1 billion fund and you took the lease fees, the transfer fees and the tokenization fees, you would get revenue of approximately $20 million with relatively modest trading levels.'' This implies that roughly half of the revenues would come from the lease spread and half from the transfer and tokenization fees.

Estimating total AUM

To calculate the potential revenue Streamex would make, we have to estimate what is the total amount of gold being leased by Monetary Metals – AUM. Based on the data that I found I believe that it is quite small. On November 12th 2024, they announced a Silver Bond financing for Bunker Hill Mining Corp for 1 million ounces (at the time of the signing, the total value of leased silver was c. $32 million). Since the announcement, Monetary Metals has not published any deal that is the same size or larger, only smaller ones. One can find all the news releases here. I will assume for my calculations that the total amount of precious metals leased is 10 times larger; thus, let’s estimate $300 million.

If we put the estimated 1% yield that STEX gets on the gold leased and AUM, we would arrive at the conclusion that STEX would make $3 million from their partnership with Monetary Metals. If we include the transfer and tokenization fees, it would be $6 million, yet these fees are not stable, especially the tokenization fee, which happens only once. This is quite low for a company currently valued at c. $500 million. This gives us a PS ratio of 160 (without including transfer and tokenization fees). I believe that based on this calculation, the company is overvalued.

''Subprime'' Lending

Monetary Metals website states that ''Full legal title and ownership of client gold always stay with the client, even when leased for a return.'' it also states that in the event of default ''Monetary Metals has legal and financial avenues to recover value''. Thus, in my opinion, a client would continue to own the digital asset, but the underlying asset gold has to be recovered from the lessee. Although the lease is insured, I believe ''subprime'' lending always carries more risk, even if insured. In my opinion this should not be overlooked.

Frank Giustra and Exchangeable shares

Frank Giustra, a Canadian billionaire, is one of the persons backing Streamex. He is a Strategic Investor and Advisor on Commodities according to the press release and according to the Proxy statement he owns 17 million shares. I view this as positive and it should give some confidence to the investor.

In September 2025, the private entity called Streamex Exchange Corp was merged into a publicly listed BioSig Technologies, creating Streamex Corp. I find it quite interesting that the total amount of the offering was only $15 million. Thus, in my opinion, the amount raised was not significant.

The second possibility would be that insiders want to offload some risk once the lockup period expires. Since the merger was effective on September, 12th 2025, the lockup period should end on March 12, 2026 (180 days is the standard length of the lockup period). This potential selling could act as downward pressure on the stock price. Thus, we need to look deeper into how many shares insiders own.

The shares outstanding count is c. 43 million shares, based on SA. But the true dilution are the Exchangable shares. According to the latest Proxy Statement, there are 109,070,079 Exchangeable Shares being held by a company called ExchangeCo. I believe that even if a small part of these shares is sold, it would create a lot of downward pressure on the stock price. I find it likely that insiders will want to take some risk off the table.

Risks to my short thesis

I believe there are two main risks to my short thesis:

First is AUM being much larger than I estimated, making STEX’s revenues higher than I estimated in the article. These revenues would also most likely be constant and high margin since STEX acts as a company connecting GLDY holders with Monetary Metals. This would make them similar to a SaaS business model.

Second is a hype cycle around tokenized real-world assets. Should such an event occur, I believe the stock price could rise significantly.

Conclusion

I rate Streamex Corp. (STEX) a sell/short. In my view, the company is currently overvalued, and there is a possibility of downward pressure once the lockup period expires. I would, however, reevaluate the company once we see some revenues materializing and once the lockup expiry event is in the past.

data.title: MLPI: Collect A Double-Digit Yield From This MLP ETF, Without The K-1 Headache
data.description:
Overview

I've held MLPs (Master Limited Partnerships) within my dividend portfolio because of their unique tax benefits and sector exposure. MLPs have the ability to offer higher than average dividend yield because they are legally required to generate at least 90% of their income from qualifying sources. This makes this a great tool within an income focused portfolio and a good fit for investors that want to limit their overall tax liability. However, I've limited my exposure to different MLPs in the last year because dealing with multiple Schedule K-1 forms during tax time can become a headache.

Neos MLP & Energy Infrastructure High Income ETF (MLPI) now offers investors a way to get direct exposure to this unique asset class, while providing diversity and eliminating K-1 form. MLPI is still relatively new with an inception dating back to December of 2025. Looking at the performance since the fund's inception, we can see that MLPI's share price has increased by about 3.2%. When including the distributions that were paid out to shareholders so far, the total return jumps up to nearly 6% over the same time frame. MLPI now offers an estimated annual dividend yield above 15% and issues payouts on a monthly basis.

NEOS has maintained an excellent track record within the high-yield option ETF space, so I am excited to see how this fund performs over a longer holding period. MLPI is able to provide this high yield due to the inclusion of an option writing strategy that's deployed against its assets. While this allows an amplified income to be collected, there are also some tradeoffs that investors should consider before initiating a position. For instance, MLPI may not be the best choice for investors looking to maximize total returns from the sector. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that MLPI implements.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, MLPI has total net assets of $110.4M that are spread across a diverse range of master limited partnerships. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 0.68%, which is fairly reasonable considering the high dividend yield being offered to investors. MLPI seeks to distribute a high monthly income from MLPs and other energy infrastructure companies. The fund also combines strategic tax loss harvesting opportunities, as well as receiving pass-through tax benefits from the MLP structure itself.

What I like about MLPI's strategy is that it specifically implements an OTM (out-of-the-money) strategy, which allows for greater upside capital appreciation. This is because an OTM strategy means that the selected strike price is typically above the current market price of the asset that the option is based on. Rather than write options against its own holdings, the fund's prospectus specifically states that it writes options against 'MLP ETFs' but it doesn't specifically list out any names. I would assume that this leaves the fund pretty flexible in terms of which ETF it can write options on, such as something like Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) or even the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE).

When looking at the top holdings of the fund, we can see that MLPI provides direct equity exposure to some of the most notable names in the sector. For instance, The Williams Companies (WMB) is the largest holding at 9.76% of assets. This is followed by exposure to many other high quality names throughout the sector, including Enbridge (ENB), TC Energy (TRP), Kinder Morgan (KMI), ONEOK (OKE), and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), just to name a few.

Although MLPI provides direct exposure to these companies, investors should limit their expectations for MLPI to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation. Despite an OTM strategy being implemented, the upside growth is still capped at whatever the selected strike price is. So if MLPI writes an option on an ETF priced at $50 per share, it may select a strike price at $55 per share. While this allows for some upside growth, MLPI will not capture any growth above the strike price.

Performance Insights & Risk

Since the fund's history is so short, we have a limited operating window to assess its performance so far. When running MLPI's performance against some ETFs like AMLP and XLE, we can see that MLPI has the lowest level of price growth over the month. So although the energy sector has run higher, this is already an indication that MLPI's structure will cap the upside growth over a prolonged holding period. In comparison, both AMLP and XLE are structured as traditional ETFs so they are able to capture the complete upside of their holdings.

When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, MLPI slightly outperforms AMLP since its inception. While MLPI's high double-digit distribution may lead to outperformance during specific market conditions, I believe that the outlook can be summarized by three scenarios. This is why MLPI may not be the best buy-and-hold investment for long-term investors that want to maximize their total return. The fund's strategy means that MLPI is likely to perform as such:

  • MLPs Rise In Value = MLPI will also rise in value since the fund has direct equity exposure to its holdings. The option writing strategy will likely produce large option premiums and distributions are most likely to stay within a consistent range. However, MLPI will underperform traditional MLP ETFs due to the nature of the capped upside.
  • MLPs Trade Flat = This is where MLPI has the best chance of outperforming traditional ETFs like AMLP. MLPI's share price may stay within a consistent range and if the option writing strategy is successful, the large premiums can result in outperformance.
  • MLPs Decline In Value = MLPI will also decline. Although the options cap upside growth, it does not protect against downside movements. While its entirely possible that distributions can soften the rate of declines, MLPI can still suffer from NAV erosion if option premiums aren't generating enough income to support distributions. Therefore, distributions can also fall if MLPs experience a prolonged decline.

Therefore, the best scenario would be for MLPs to trade mostly flat or slightly increase over time. If MLPs rise too fast, MLPI will severely underperform over a longer holding period. Additionally, the fund will struggle to recover from any sharp drops in the MLP market, since the option writing strategy caps upside growth. So to manage these risks, I believe that MLPI is most ideal when used as an accompanying position for an investor that already has exposure to MLPs, whether that's through an ETF like AMLP or individual holdings.

By implementing the fund with this approach, investors that want to grow their capital can better participate during bull markets. Additionally, MLPI's diversity can also act as a hedge during unfavorable market cycles since it eliminates single stock risk. Plus, MLPs tend to demonstrate better resistance against cyclical shifts in the market compared to traditional growth stocks.

Tax-Efficient Dividend Potential

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.6608 per share, the estimated forward annual dividend yield sits around 15.4%. Since the fund has only issued two distributions since its inception, we can only calculate the dividend yield on a forward basis. Unfortunately, we have no history to boldly claim that MLPI will offer excellent dividend consistency over time. However, I anticipate that NEOS will follow the same priority of consistent payouts as it does with its other covered call ETFs. I plan to keep track of the payouts over the next year and will revisit the fund after we have an established payout history.

The primary risk for the MLPI is a scenario where the sector experiences a downturn. If the fund finds itself in a position where it is frequently paying out more than it earns, the distributions will have to be adjusted downward as the underlying NAV of the fund declines. This is just the nature of these covered call ETFs that rely on option premiums to fund distributions, so this is a risk that investors should be aware of.

On a more positive note, one of the main benefits of MLPs is their unique tax structure that allows investors to defer taxes of all dividends received. However, this usually involves investors to receive a Schedule K-1 Form that must be reported during tax time. Having filled these out for several years, they can be a headache but the most frustrating part is that these forms mailed out in mid-March. So investors need to delay the filing of their taxes and this leaves little room for planning.

MLPI captures a similar benefit but by strategically using return of capital distributions. Unlike traditional distributions supported by net investment income, return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. Referring to the latest section 19(a) notice, 100% of the distributions paid so far have been classified as return of capital.

This makes MLPI an excellent choice for investors that want exposure to MLPs and their tax-benefits, but don't want to deal with Schedule K-1 Forms. This also enables investors some flexibility in terms of which investment account they can utilize MLPI in. Investors that decide to hold MLPI within a regular taxable brokerage account may be able to effectively defer taxes for a long period of time. In theory, it's possible to hold MLPI for long enough until an investor's cost basis reaches $0 per share. At this point, all distributions received would be classified as long-term capital gains, which are still tax-efficient.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that MLPI is a compelling alternative for investors that want exposure to MLPs without the hassle of the added paperwork at tax time. The fund provides direct equity exposure to a diverse range of MLPs, while implementing a covered call strategy against MLP ETFs. While this can cap the fund's upside growth potential, it also allows MLPI to generate an amplified level of income during times where the sector is trading flat. Additionally, MLPI's focus on return of capital distributions means that the distributions received are still considered to be tax-efficient over time. Therefore, I believe that MLPI can be a solid long-term holding for investors looking to minimize their tax liability.

data.title: U.S. Fixed Income: Policy-Driven Opportunities Emerge
data.description:
By Ashok Bhatia, CFA and Jason W. Smith

Amid the administration’s flurry of affordability-targeted policy initiatives, bond investment opportunities are emerging.

Episodes like last week’s geopolitical flare-up over Greenland crank up the global macro noise and volatility that have been with us for some time, making it easy to be distracted by headlines. Yet as similar bouts of short-term market turbulence emerge through the year, as we expect, the real challenge is to stay anchored in fundamentals, focused on long-term outcomes and attentive to smaller policy developments at home that can move markets in more durable—and investable—ways.

In U.S. fixed income, we are seeing this play out in certain asset classes—most notably agency mortgage‑backed securities (MBS), where the opportunity, in our view, is worth focusing on.

Several forces are at work: the Federal Reserve’s easing bias, still-elevated (if tighter) spreads and improving technicals. The most powerful, however, is the administration’s recently announced $200 billion MBS purchase program to be executed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, the government-sponsored enterprises (GSEs) at the center of the U.S. mortgage market.

Announced on January 8, the program is essentially designed to help support U.S. households and stimulate the housing market by creating greater demand for agency MBS, thereby compressing mortgage spreads and pushing mortgage rates lower.

In isolation, this may look like a sizable initiative, but against the backdrop of the much bigger macro shifts underway in the U.S. and globally, it could easily be overlooked—precisely why we think it deserves closer attention.

Why We See an Opportunity Now

The reason is simple: A large, policy-driven buyer is returning to a market where many investors continue to seek high-quality fixed income.

Even before the formal announcement, markets had begun to anticipate that the GSEs would become more active buyers. In the fourth quarter of 2025, agency MBS spreads started to tighten as investors increasingly priced in additional Fannie and Freddie demand. The January announcement has now turned that expectation into an explicit commitment.

For the past couple of years, agency MBS have mainly been bought by spread-sensitive managers rotating in because mortgages looked cheap versus corporate bonds and other credit. Now, with the administration signaling that Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac will use their balance sheets more aggressively, the marginal buyer is shifting from opportunistic private capital to steady public sector demand. When that buyer’s primary mandate is to own these bonds rather than to trade relative value, spreads tend not just to tighten but to remain contained, if not grind tighter.

Scale also matters. The $200 billion headline broadly matches the remaining capacity the GSEs have under current regulatory caps. If more were deemed necessary, raising those caps is at least a plausible next step. That possibility is one reason the market views more consistent support as the risk rather than less consistent support.

All this is happening while spreads, though off their wides, still sit above longer-term averages, and interest rate volatility has declined as the Fed’s path has become clearer. In our view, that mix of supportive policy, still-attractive risk premia and a calmer rates backdrop creates a sensible entry point to add high-quality fixed income before the value becomes more widely recognized.

Balancing Quality and Income

We do not expect this program to transform U.S. housing or send mortgage rates sharply lower on its own. Treasury yields will remain the main driver of what borrowers pay.

Instead, we expect something more measured and investable: spreads moving from “cheap” toward “fair”, and possibly a little richer, as the program is implemented and potentially extended. That gradual tightening can be uncomfortable for investors waiting for a pullback, but it can be rewarding for those prepared to own the sector and let carry work over time.

Importantly, agency MBS also continue to offer a compelling balance of quality and income. In a world where credit risk has been quiet but is starting to build, they remain a high-quality, government-backed option for investors who want fixed income without corporate credit exposure. The risk premium is not as generous as it was at its widest, but we believe it remains attractive given the underlying support.

One word of caution, low interest rate volatility has been a tailwind for the sector. If volatility were to rise again—whether because of the Fed, the long end of the Treasury market or external shocks—that would matter for mortgages. Being clear about where we are in the rate volatility cycle is therefore a key part of our risk assessment.

Leaning Into the Backstop

With credit risk likely to become more of a focus over time, we think investors should pay attention when a high-quality, government-backed asset class gains a fresh source of official support. Agency MBS are no longer outright cheap and the Fed’s rate-cutting cycle is more advanced, but that is exactly why this phase is about locking in resilient income where fundamentals, policy and market technicals are aligned.

We believe U.S. agency MBS now sits firmly in that category. Our approach is to be prudent but active: selective on structure and coupon, alert to prepayment and interest rate volatility risks, yet willing to build exposure into a market where public sector demand is likely to do much of the heavy lifting.

What to Watch For

  • Monday 1/26:
    • U.S. Durable Goods Order
    • U.S. Atlanta Fed GDPNow
  • Tuesday 1/27:
    • U.S. CB Consumer Confidence
  • Wednesday 1/28:
    • Canada BoC Interest Rate Decision
    • U.S. Federal Reserve Interest Rate Decision
  • Thursday 1/29:
    • U.S. Initial Jobless Claims
  • Friday 1/30:
    • Eurozone GDP
    • Eurozone Unemployment Rate
    • U.S. Producer Price Index
